'''祭神'''（さいじん）とは、ある[[神社]]について、そこに祀られている[[神 (神道)|神]]を指す言葉である。
==歴史== 現在、多くの神社では、[[日本神話]]に登場する神を祭神としているか、日本神話の神と同神であるとしている。
元々[[神道]]は海・山・川などを[[神体]]とする自然崇拝から始まったものであり、初期の神社では、そこに祀られる神には特に名前はないか、不詳であった。[[記紀]]や[[万葉集]]などでも、祭神の名が記されているのは[[伊勢神宮]]、[[住吉神社]]などごくわずかであり、ほとんどの神社の祭神は、鎮座地名や神社名に「神」をつけただけの名前で呼ばれていた。[[延喜式神名帳]]でもほとんどの神社は社名しか記されていないことから、延喜式が編まれた10世紀初頭ごろまではほとんどの神社の祭神には特に名前がついていなかったことがわかる。
10世紀ごろから、それまでの[[氏神]]・[[地主神]]としての性格だけでなく、火の神・水の神・木の神などの具体的な神徳・機能が附加されるようになり、祭神も、その神徳に合わせて地名・社名から日本神話に登場する神、あるいは「命」「彦」「比売」などをつけた人格的な神に移行するようになった。また、稲荷・八幡などの有力な神を[[勧請]]してそれを主神とすることも広く行われた。
==主神・配神== 通常、神社では複数の神を祀っており、その中で主として祀られる神を'''主神'''（しゅしん）・'''主祭神'''（しゅさいじん）、それ以外の神を'''配神'''（はいしん）・'''配祀神'''（はいししん）・'''相殿神'''（あいどのしん）などという。
祭神を主神・配神に分けるのは、明治時代に官国幣社で行われるようになったのに始まるものであるが、「主神とそれ以外の神」という観念はそれ以前からあり、「前」、「相殿神」などと呼ばれていた。配神は通常は主神に縁のある神であるが、その他様々な経緯により共に祀られるようになった配神もある。主神と同時に祀られるようになった配神もあれば、後で加えられた配神もある。中には、元々は配神であったのが、後に主神に取って変わったものもある。明治時代の[[神社合祀]]により、多くの配神を祀ることになった神社もある。
'''相殿'''（あいどの。合殿とも書く）とは、主神を含めて複数の神が祀られた[[社殿]]のことを指す。「相殿神」とは相殿に祀られる神のことであるが、主神と配神とがある場合は配神のことを相殿神という。
[[Category:神道|さいしん]] {{shinto-stub}}

'''相殿'''（あいどの。合殿とも書く）とは、主神を含めて複数の神が祀られた[[社殿]]のことを指す。「相殿神」とは相殿に祀られる神のことであるが、主神と配神とがある場合は配神のことを相殿神という。

{{出典の明記|date=2011年3月}} {{神道}} '''祭神'''（さいじん）とは、ある[[神社]]について、そこに祀られている[[神 (神道)|神]]を指す言葉である。
==歴史== 現在、多くの神社では、[[日本神話]]に登場する神を祭神としているか、日本神話の神と同神であるとしている。
元々[[神道]]は海・山・川などを畏敬の対象の[[神体]]とする自然崇拝から始まったものであり、初期の神社では、そこに祀られる神には特に名前はないか、不詳であった。[[記紀]]や[[万葉集]]などでも、祭神の名が記されているのは[[伊勢神宮]]、[[住吉神社]]などごくわずかであり、ほとんどの神社の祭神は、鎮座地名や神社名に「神」をつけただけの名前で呼ばれていた。[[延喜式神名帳]]でもほとんどの神社は社名しか記されていないことから、延喜式が編まれた10世紀初頭ごろまではほとんどの神社の祭神には特に名前がついていなかったことがわかる。
10世紀ごろから、それまでの[[氏神]]・[[地主神]]・[[岐の神]]としての性格だけでなく、火の神・水の神・木の神などの具体的な神徳・機能が附加されるようになった。鎌倉時代末期になると、[[仏教]]による[[本地垂迹説]]に対する[[本地垂迹#反本地垂迹説|神本仏迹説]]が主に武家に支持されて隆盛となり、祭神も、その神徳に合わせて地名・社名から日本神話に登場する神、あるいは「命・尊」「彦・比古」「姫・媛・比売」などをつけた人格的な神に移行するようになった。また、稲荷・八幡などの有力な神を[[分霊]]（[[勧請]]）してそれを主神とすることも広く行われた。この流れは江戸末期の[[国学者]]たちによる[[復古神道]]の提唱により[[神典]]が頻繁に引用されて行われ、さらに明治になり[[神仏分離]]として結実すると、村社末社無格社に至るまで浸透した。
そのため、神社の由緒には確実性に疑問が残ることとなり、式内社の[[論社]]においても伝承により後裔の可能性がきわめて高い論社という扱いとなっている。
==主神・配神== 通常、神社では複数の神を祀っており、その中で主として祀られる神を'''主神'''（しゅしん）・'''主祭神'''（しゅさいじん）、それ以外の神を'''配神'''（はいしん）・'''配祀神'''（はいししん）・'''相殿神'''（あいどのしん）などという。
祭神を主神・配神に分けるのは、明治時代に官国幣社で行われるようになったのに始まるものであるが、「主神とそれ以外の神」という観念はそれ以前からあり、「前」、「相殿神」などと呼ばれていた。配神は通常は主神に縁のある神であるが、その他様々な経緯により共に祀られるようになった配神もある。主神と同時に祀られるようになった配神もあれば、後で加えられた配神もある。中には、元々は配神であったのが、後に主神に取って変わったものもある。明治時代の[[神社合祀]]により、多くの配神を祀ることになった神社もある。
'''相殿'''（あいどの。合殿とも書く）とは、主神を含めて複数の神が祀られた[[社殿]]のことを指す。「相殿神」とは相殿に祀られる神のことであるが、主神と配神とがある場合は配神のことを相殿神という。

{{出典の明記|date=2011年3月}} {{神道}} '''祭神'''（さいじん）とは、ある[[神社]]について、そこに祀られている[[神 (神道)|神]]を指す言葉である。
==歴史== 現在、多くの神社では、[[日本神話]]に登場する神を祭神としているか、日本神話の神と同神であるとしている。
元々[[神道]]は海・山・川などを畏敬の対象の[[神体]]とする自然崇拝から始まったものであり、初期の神社では、そこに祀られる神には特に名前はないか、不詳であった。[[記紀]]や[[万葉集]]などでも、祭神の名が記されているのは[[伊勢神宮]]、[[住吉神社]]などごくわずかであり、ほとんどの神社の祭神は、鎮座地名や神社名に「神」をつけただけの名前で呼ばれていた。[[延喜式神名帳]]でもほとんどの神社は社名しか記されていないことから、延喜式が編まれた10世紀初頭ごろまではほとんどの神社の祭神には特に名前がついていなかったことがわかる。
10世紀ごろから、それまでの[[氏神]]・[[地主神]]・[[岐の神]]としての性格だけでなく、火の神・水の神・木の神などの具体的な神徳・機能が附加されるようになった。鎌倉時代末期になると、[[仏教]]による[[本地垂迹説]]に対する[[本地垂迹#反本地垂迹説|神本仏迹説]]が主に武家に支持されて隆盛となり、祭神も、その神徳に合わせて地名・社名から日本神話に登場する神、あるいは「命・尊」「彦・比古」「姫・媛・比売」などをつけた人格的な神に移行するようになった。また、稲荷・八幡などの有力な神を[[分霊]]（[[勧請]]）してそれを主神とすることも広く行われた。この流れは江戸末期の[[国学者]]たちによる[[復古神道]]の提唱により[[神典]]が頻繁に引用されて行われ、さらに明治になり[[神仏分離]]として結実すると、村社末社無格社に至るまで浸透した。
そのため、神社の由緒には確実性に疑問が残ることとなり、式内社の[[論社]]においても伝承により後裔の可能性がきわめて高い論社という扱いとなっている。

祭神を主神・配神に分けるのは、明治時代に官国幣社で行われるようになったのに始まるものである。「主神とそれ以外の神」という観念はそれ以前からあり、「前」「相殿神」などと呼ばれていた。配神は通常は主神にゆかりのある神だが、そのほか様々な経緯により共に祀られるようになった配神もある。主神と同時に祀られるようになった配神もあれば、後で加えられた配神もあり、中には本来は配神だったのが後に主神に取って替わったものもある。明治時代の[[神社合祀]]により、多くの配神を祀ることになった神社もある。
相殿（合殿とも）とは、主神を含めて複数の神が祀られた[[社殿]]のことを指す。「相殿神」とは相殿に祀られる神のことだが、主神と配神とがある場合は配神のことを相殿神という。

相殿（合殿とも）とは、主神を含めて複数の神が祀られた[[社殿]]のことを指す。「相殿神」とは相殿に祀られる神のことだが、主神と配神とがある場合は配神のことを相殿神という。
==関連項目== *[[神体]] *[[神像]] *[[本尊]] *[[人神]]
{{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:さいしん}} [[Category:神道]] {{shinto-stub}}

{{出典の明記|date=2011年3月}} '''祭神'''（さいじん）とは、その[[神社]]について、そこに祀られている[[神 (神道)|神]]を指す言葉である。
==歴史== 現在、多くの神社では、[[日本神話]]に登場する神を祭神としているか、日本神話の神と同神であるとしている。
元々[[神道]]は海・山・川などを畏敬の対象の[[神体]]とする自然崇拝から始まったものであり、初期の神社では、そこに祀られる神には特に名前はないか、不詳であった。[[記紀]]や[[万葉集]]などでも、祭神の名が記されているのは[[伊勢神宮]]、[[住吉神社]]などごくわずかであり、ほとんどの神社の祭神は、鎮座地名や神社名に「神」をつけただけの名前で呼ばれていた。[[延喜式神名帳]]でもほとんどの神社は社名しか記されていないことから、延喜式が編まれた10世紀初頭ごろまではほとんどの神社の祭神には特に名前がついていなかったことがわかる。
10世紀ごろから、それまでの[[氏神]]・[[地主神]]・[[岐の神]]としての性格だけでなく、火の神・水の神・木の神などの具体的な神徳・機能が附加されるようになった。鎌倉時代末期になると、[[仏教]]による[[本地垂迹説]]に対する[[本地垂迹#反本地垂迹説|神本仏迹説]]が主に武家に支持されて隆盛となり、祭神も、その神徳に合わせて地名・社名から日本神話に登場する神、あるいは「神」「命・尊」「彦・比古」「姫・媛・比売」などをつけた人格的な神に移行するようになった。また、稲荷・八幡などの有力な神を[[分霊]]（[[勧請]]）してそれを主神とすることも広く行われた。この流れは江戸末期の[[国学者]]たちによる[[復古神道]]の提唱により[[神典]]が頻繁に引用されて行われ、さらに明治になり[[神仏分離]]として結実すると、村社末社無格社に至るまで浸透した。
そのため、神社の由緒には確実性に疑問が残ることとなり、式内社の[[論社]]においても伝承により後裔の可能性がきわめて高い論社という扱いとなっている。
==主神・配神== 通常、神社では複数の神を祀っており、その中で主として祀られる神を'''主神'''（しゅしん）・'''主祭神'''（しゅさいじん）、それ以外の神を'''配神'''（はいしん）・'''配祀神'''（はいししん）・'''相殿神'''（あいどのしん）などという。
祭神を主神・配神に分けるのは、明治時代に官国幣社で行われるようになったのに始まるものである。「主神とそれ以外の神」という観念はそれ以前からあり、「前」「相殿神」などと呼ばれていた。配神は通常は主神にゆかりのある神だが、そのほか様々な経緯により共に祀られるようになった配神もある。主神と同時に祀られるようになった配神もあれば、後で加えられた配神もあり、中には本来は配神だったのが後に主神に取って替わったものもある。明治時代の[[神社合祀]]により、多くの配神を祀ることになった神社もある。
相殿（合殿とも）とは、主神を含めて複数の神が祀られた[[社殿]]のことを指す。「相殿神」とは相殿に祀られる神のことだが、主神と配神とがある場合は配神のことを相殿神という。

revision.text:
{{Cite sources|date=March 2011}} '''Saijin''' (祭神) refers to the {{ill|deity|ja|神}} enshrined at a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.
== History == Currently, most shrines enshrine deities from {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話}}, or identify their deities as equivalent to those from the mythology.
Originally, {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} began as a form of nature worship, revering sacred objects like seas, mountains, and rivers as {{ill|shintai|ja|神体}} (sacred objects). In early shrines, the enshrined deities often had no specific names or were unknown. In texts like the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, and the {{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}, only a few shrines, such as {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|住吉神社}}, mention the names of their deities. Most shrines referred to their deities simply by appending "kami" to the shrine or place name. For example, the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}} lists mostly shrine names without specifying the names of the deities, suggesting that by the early 10th century, most deities did not have formal names.
From around the 10th century, deities began to be associated with specific functions or virtues, such as fire, water, or wood deities, beyond their original roles as tutelary gods of clans or regions. By the late {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the emergence of {{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|ja|本地垂迹説}} (theory of the Buddha as the true nature of kami) and its counterpart, {{ill|Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja|反本地垂迹説}} (Shinto superiority), spurred the personalization of deities. Names were often derived from Japanese mythology, local legends, or combinations of honorifics like "kami" (god), "mikoto" (honorific suffix), "hiko" (prince), or "hime" (princess). Additionally, influential deities such as {{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷}} and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡}} were frequently {{ill|divided spirits|ja|分霊}} and enshrined as main deities.
This trend continued into the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, influenced by the {{ill|Restoration Shinto|ja|復古神道}} movement. By the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) formalized this process, leading to widespread standardization of shrine practices and deity names, even at local and minor shrines.
However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Shikinai shrines|ja|式内社}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).
The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
== Related Terms == * {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体}} * {{ill|Shinzou|ja|神像}} * {{ill|Honzon|ja|本尊}} * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神}}
{{Shinto Horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Saijin}} [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump]] [[ja:祭神]]

For example, the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}} lists mostly shrine names without specifying the names of the deities, suggesting that by the early 10th century, most deities did not have formal names.
From around the 10th century, deities began to be associated with specific functions or virtues, such as fire, water, or wood deities, beyond their original roles as tutelary gods of clans or regions. By the late {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the emergence of {{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|ja|本地垂迹説}} (theory of the Buddha as the true nature of kami) and its counterpart, {{ill|Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja|反本地垂迹説}} (Shinto superiority), spurred the personalization of deities. Names were often derived from Japanese mythology, local legends, or combinations of honorifics like "kami" (god), "mikoto" (honorific suffix), "hiko" (prince), or "hime" (princess). Additionally, influential deities such as {{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷}} and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡}} were frequently {{ill|divided spirits|ja|分霊}} and enshrined as main deities.
This trend continued into the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, influenced by the {{ill|Restoration Shinto|ja|復古神道}} movement. By the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) formalized this process, leading to widespread standardization of shrine practices and deity names, even at local and minor shrines.
However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Shikinai shrines|ja|式内社}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).
The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
== Related Terms == * {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体}} * {{ill|Shinzou|ja|神像}} * {{ill|Honzon|ja|本尊}} * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神}}

The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.

{{Cite sources|date=March 2011}} '''Saijin''' (祭神) refers to the {{ill|deity|ja|神}} enshrined at a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.
== History == Currently, most shrines enshrine deities from {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話}}, or identify their deities as equivalent to those from the mythology.
Originally, {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} began as a form of nature worship, revering sacred objects like seas, mountains, and rivers as {{ill|shintai|ja|神体}} (sacred objects). In early shrines, the enshrined deities often had no specific names or were unknown. In texts like the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, and the {{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}, only a few shrines, such as {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|住吉神社}}, mention the names of their deities. Most shrines referred to their deities simply by appending "kami" to the shrine or place name. For example, the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}} lists mostly shrine names without specifying the names of the deities, suggesting that by the early 10th century, most deities did not have formal names.
From around the 10th century, deities began to be associated with specific functions or virtues, such as fire, water, or wood deities, beyond their original roles as tutelary gods of clans or regions. By the late {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the emergence of {{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|ja|本地垂迹説}} (theory of the Buddha as the true nature of kami) and its counterpart, {{ill|Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja|反本地垂迹説}} (Shinto superiority), spurred the personalization of deities. Names were often derived from Japanese mythology, local legends, or combinations of honorifics like "kami" (god), "mikoto" (honorific suffix), "hiko" (prince), or "hime" (princess). Additionally, influential deities such as {{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷}} and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡}} were frequently {{ill|divided spirits|ja|分霊}} and enshrined as main deities.
This trend continued into the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, influenced by the {{ill|Restoration Shinto|ja|復古神道}} movement. By the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) formalized this process, leading to widespread standardization of shrine practices and deity names, even at local and minor shrines.
However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Shikinai shrines|ja|式内社}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).
The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
== Related Terms == * {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体}} * {{ill|Shinzou|ja|神像}} * {{ill|Honzon|ja|本尊}} * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神}}

{{Cite sources|date=March 2011}} '''Saijin''' (祭神) refers to the {{ill|deity|ja|神}} enshrined at a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.
== History == Currently, most shrines enshrine deities from {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話}}, or identify their deities as equivalent to those from the mythology.
Originally, {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} began as a form of nature worship, revering sacred objects like seas, mountains, and rivers as {{ill|shintai|ja|神体}} (sacred objects). In early shrines, the enshrined deities often had no specific names or were unknown. In texts like the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, and the {{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}, only a few shrines, such as {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|住吉神社}}, mention the names of their deities. Most shrines referred to their deities simply by appending "kami" to the shrine or place name. For example, the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}} lists mostly shrine names without specifying the names of the deities, suggesting that by the early 10th century, most deities did not have formal names.
From around the 10th century, deities began to be associated with specific functions or virtues, such as fire, water, or wood deities, beyond their original roles as tutelary gods of clans or regions. By the late {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the emergence of {{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|ja|本地垂迹説}} (theory of the Buddha as the true nature of kami) and its counterpart, {{ill|Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja|反本地垂迹説}} (Shinto superiority), spurred the personalization of deities. Names were often derived from Japanese mythology, local legends, or combinations of honorifics like "kami" (god), "mikoto" (honorific suffix), "hiko" (prince), or "hime" (princess). Additionally, influential deities such as {{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷}} and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡}} were frequently {{ill|divided spirits|ja|分霊}} and enshrined as main deities.
This trend continued into the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, influenced by the {{ill|Restoration Shinto|ja|復古神道}} movement. By the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) formalized this process, leading to widespread standardization of shrine practices and deity names, even at local and minor shrines.
However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Shikinai shrines|ja|式内社}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).
The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
== Related Terms == * {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体}} * {{ill|Shinzou|ja|神像}} * {{ill|Honzon|ja|本尊}} * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神}}
{{Shinto Horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Saijin}} [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[ja:祭神]]

This trend continued into the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, influenced by the {{ill|Restoration Shinto|ja|復古神道}} movement. Additionally, influential deities such as {{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷}} and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡}} were frequently {{ill|divided spirits|ja|分霊}} and enshrined as main deities.
This trend continued into the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, influenced by the {{ill|Restoration Shinto|ja|復古神道}} movement. By the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) formalized this process, leading to widespread standardization of shrine practices and deity names, even at local and minor shrines.
However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Shikinai shrines|ja|式内社}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).
The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
== Related Terms == * {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体}} * {{ill|Shinzou|ja|神像}} * {{ill|Honzon|ja|本尊}} * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神}}

{{Cite sources|date=March 2011}} '''Saijin''' (祭神) refers to the {{ill|deity|ja|神}} enshrined at a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.
== History == Currently, most shrines enshrine deities from {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話}}, or identify their deities as equivalent to those from the mythology.
Originally, {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} began as a form of nature worship, revering sacred objects like seas, mountains, and rivers as {{ill|shintai|ja|神体}} (sacred objects). In early shrines, the enshrined deities often had no specific names or were unknown. In texts like the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, and the {{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}, only a few shrines, such as {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|住吉神社}}, mention the names of their deities. Most shrines referred to their deities simply by appending "kami" to the shrine or place name. For example, the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}} lists mostly shrine names without specifying the names of the deities, suggesting that by the early 10th century, most deities did not have formal names.
From around the 10th century, deities began to be associated with specific functions or virtues, such as fire, water, or wood deities, beyond their original roles as tutelary gods of clans or regions. By the late {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the emergence of {{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|ja|本地垂迹説}} (theory of the Buddha as the true nature of kami) and its counterpart, {{ill|Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja|反本地垂迹説}} (Shinto superiority), spurred the personalization of deities. Names were often derived from Japanese mythology, local legends, or combinations of honorifics like "kami" (god), "mikoto" (honorific suffix), "hiko" (prince), or "hime" (princess). Additionally, influential deities such as {{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷}} and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡}} were frequently {{ill|divided spirits|ja|分霊}} and enshrined as main deities.
This trend continued into the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, influenced by the {{ill|Fukko Shinto|ja|復古神道|Fukko Shinto=Fukko Shinto|lt=Restoration Shinto}} movement. By the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) formalized this process, leading to widespread standardization of shrine practices and deity names, even at local and minor shrines.
However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Shikinai shrines|ja|式内社}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).

Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.

{{Cite sources|date=March 2011}} '''Saijin''' (祭神) refers to the {{ill|deity|ja|神}} enshrined at a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.
== History == Currently, most shrines enshrine deities from {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話}}, or identify their deities as equivalent to those from the mythology.
Originally, {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} began as a form of nature worship, revering sacred objects like seas, mountains, and rivers as {{ill|shintai|ja|神体}} (sacred objects). In early shrines, the enshrined deities often had no specific names or were unknown. In texts like the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, and the {{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}, only a few shrines, such as {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|住吉神社}}, mention the names of their deities. Most shrines referred to their deities simply by appending "kami" to the shrine or place name. For example, the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}} lists mostly shrine names without specifying the names of the deities, suggesting that by the early 10th century, most deities did not have formal names.
From around the 10th century, deities began to be associated with specific functions or virtues, such as fire, water, or wood deities, beyond their original roles as tutelary gods of clans or regions. By the late {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the emergence of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹説|Honji suijaku=Honji suijaku|lt=Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu}} (theory of the Buddha as the true nature of kami) and its counterpart, {{ill|Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja|反本地垂迹説}} (Shinto superiority), spurred the personalization of deities. Names were often derived from Japanese mythology, local legends, or combinations of honorifics like "kami" (god), "mikoto" (honorific suffix), "hiko" (prince), or "hime" (princess). Additionally, influential deities such as {{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷}} and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡}} were frequently {{ill|divided spirits|ja|分霊}} and enshrined as main deities.
This trend continued into the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, influenced by the {{ill|Fukko Shinto|ja|復古神道|Fukko Shinto=Fukko Shinto|lt=Restoration Shinto}} movement. By the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) formalized this process, leading to widespread standardization of shrine practices and deity names, even at local and minor shrines.
However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Shikinai shrines|ja|式内社}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).
The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
== Related Terms == * {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体}} * {{ill|Shinzou|ja|神像}} * {{ill|Honzon|ja|本尊}} * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神}}

By the late {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the emergence of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹説|Honji suijaku=Honji suijaku|lt=Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu}} (theory of the Buddha as the true nature of kami) and its counterpart, {{ill|Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja|反本地垂迹説}} (Shinto superiority), spurred the personalization of deities. Names were often derived from Japanese mythology, local legends, or combinations of honorifics like "kami" (god), "mikoto" (honorific suffix), "hiko" (prince), or "hime" (princess). Additionally, influential deities such as {{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷}} and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡}} were frequently {{ill|divided spirits|ja|分霊}} and enshrined as main deities.
This trend continued into the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, influenced by the {{ill|Fukko Shinto|ja|復古神道|Fukko Shinto=Fukko Shinto|lt=Restoration Shinto}} movement. By the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) formalized this process, leading to widespread standardization of shrine practices and deity names, even at local and minor shrines.
However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Shikinai shrines|ja|式内社}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).
The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.

However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Shikinai shrines|ja|式内社}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).
The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
== Related Terms == * {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体}} * {{ill|Shinzou|ja|神像}} * {{ill|Honzon|ja|本尊}} * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神}}

{{Cite sources|date=March 2011}} '''Saijin''' (祭神) refers to the {{ill|deity|ja|神}} enshrined at a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.
== History == Currently, most shrines enshrine deities from {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話}}, or identify their deities as equivalent to those from the mythology.
Originally, {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} began as a form of nature worship, revering sacred objects like seas, mountains, and rivers as {{ill|shintai|ja|神体}} (sacred objects). In early shrines, the enshrined deities often had no specific names or were unknown. In texts like the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, and the {{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}, only a few shrines, such as {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|住吉神社}}, mention the names of their deities. Most shrines referred to their deities simply by appending "kami" to the shrine or place name. For example, the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}} lists mostly shrine names without specifying the names of the deities, suggesting that by the early 10th century, most deities did not have formal names.
From around the 10th century, deities began to be associated with specific functions or virtues, such as fire, water, or wood deities, beyond their original roles as tutelary gods of clans or regions. By the late {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the emergence of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹説|Honji suijaku=Honji suijaku|lt=Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu}} (theory of the Buddha as the true nature of kami) and its counterpart, {{ill|Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja|反本地垂迹説}} (Shinto superiority), spurred the personalization of deities. Names were often derived from Japanese mythology, local legends, or combinations of honorifics like "kami" (god), "mikoto" (honorific suffix), "hiko" (prince), or "hime" (princess). Additionally, influential deities such as {{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷}} and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡}} were frequently {{ill|divided spirits|ja|分霊}} and enshrined as main deities.
This trend continued into the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, influenced by the {{ill|Fukko Shinto|ja|復古神道|Fukko Shinto=Fukko Shinto|lt=Restoration Shinto}} movement. By the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) formalized this process, leading to widespread standardization of shrine practices and deity names, even at local and minor shrines.
However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Shikinai shrines|ja|式内社}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).
The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
== Related Terms == * {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体}} * {{ill|Shinzou|ja|神像}} * {{ill|Honzon|ja|本尊}} * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神}}

Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
== Related Terms == * {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体}} * {{ill|Shinzou|ja|神像}} * {{ill|Honzon|ja|本尊}} * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神}}

revision.text:
{{Cite sources|date=March 2011}} '''Saijin''' (祭神) refers to the {{ill|deity|ja|神}} enshrined at a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.
== History == Currently, most shrines enshrine deities from {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話}}, or identify their deities as equivalent to those from the mythology.
Originally, {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} began as a form of nature worship, revering sacred objects like seas, mountains, and rivers as {{ill|shintai|ja|神体}} (sacred objects). In early shrines, the enshrined deities often had no specific names or were unknown. In texts like the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, and the {{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}, only a few shrines, such as {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|住吉神社}}, mention the names of their deities. Most shrines referred to their deities simply by appending "kami" to the shrine or place name. For example, the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}} lists mostly shrine names without specifying the names of the deities, suggesting that by the early 10th century, most deities did not have formal names.
From around the 10th century, deities began to be associated with specific functions or virtues, such as fire, water, or wood deities, beyond their original roles as tutelary gods of clans or regions. By the late {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the emergence of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹説|Honji suijaku=Honji suijaku|lt=Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu}} (theory of the Buddha as the true nature of kami) and its counterpart, {{ill|Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja|反本地垂迹説}} (Shinto superiority), spurred the personalization of deities. Names were often derived from Japanese mythology, local legends, or combinations of honorifics like "kami" (god), "mikoto" (honorific suffix), "hiko" (prince), or "hime" (princess). Additionally, influential deities such as {{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷}} and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡}} were frequently {{ill|divided spirits|ja|分霊}} and enshrined as main deities.
This trend continued into the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, influenced by the {{ill|Fukko Shinto|ja|復古神道|Fukko Shinto=Fukko Shinto|lt=Restoration Shinto}} movement. By the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) formalized this process, leading to widespread standardization of shrine practices and deity names, even at local and minor shrines.
However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Shikinai shrines|ja|式内社}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).
The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
== Related Terms == * {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体}} * {{ill|Shinzou|ja|神像}} * {{ill|Honzon|ja|本尊}} * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神}}
{{Shinto Horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Saijin}} [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Enshrined deity]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Saijin]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Saijin]][[Category:qq]] <!--Saijin-->[[Category:Enshrined deity]][[Category:Enshrined deity]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Saijin]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Saijin]][[Category:qq]] <!--Saijin-->[[Category:Enshrined deity]][[Category:bump]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:q--q]] [[sn:Saijin]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saijin]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saijin]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Saijin]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saijin]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saijin]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Saijin]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Saijin]][[ja:祭神]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Saijin]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Saijin]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqqq]]

revision.text:
{{Cite sources|date=March 2011}} '''Saijin''' (祭神) refers to the {{ill|deity|ja|神}} enshrined at a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.
== History == Currently, most shrines enshrine deities from {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話}}, or identify their deities as equivalent to those from the mythology.
Originally, {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} began as a form of nature worship, revering sacred objects like seas, mountains, and rivers as {{ill|shintai|ja|神体}} (sacred objects). In early shrines, the enshrined deities often had no specific names or were unknown. In texts like the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, and the {{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}, only a few shrines, such as {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|住吉神社}}, mention the names of their deities. Most shrines referred to their deities simply by appending "kami" to the shrine or place name. For example, the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}} lists mostly shrine names without specifying the names of the deities, suggesting that by the early 10th century, most deities did not have formal names.
From around the 10th century, deities began to be associated with specific functions or virtues, such as fire, water, or wood deities, beyond their original roles as tutelary gods of clans or regions. By the late {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the emergence of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹説|Honji suijaku=Honji suijaku|lt=Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu}} (theory of the Buddha as the true nature of kami) and its counterpart, {{ill|Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja|反本地垂迹説}} (Shinto superiority), spurred the personalization of deities. Names were often derived from Japanese mythology, local legends, or combinations of honorifics like "kami" (god), "mikoto" (honorific suffix), "hiko" (prince), or "hime" (princess). Additionally, influential deities such as {{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷}} and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡}} were frequently {{ill|divided spirits|ja|分霊}} and enshrined as main deities.
This trend continued into the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, influenced by the {{ill|Fukko Shinto|ja|復古神道|Fukko Shinto=Fukko Shinto|lt=Restoration Shinto}} movement. By the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) formalized this process, leading to widespread standardization of shrine practices and deity names, even at local and minor shrines.
However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Shikinai shrines|ja|式内社}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).
The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
== Related Terms == * {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体}} * {{ill|Shinzou|ja|神像}} * {{ill|Honzon|ja|本尊}} * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神}}

Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
== Related Terms == * {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体}} * {{ill|Shinzou|ja|神像}} * {{ill|Honzon|ja|本尊}} * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神}}

{{Cite sources|date=March 2011}} '''Saijin''' (祭神) refers to the {{ill|deity|ja|神}} enshrined at a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.
== History == Currently, most shrines enshrine deities from {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話}}, or identify their deities as equivalent to those from the mythology.
Originally, {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} began as a form of nature worship, revering sacred objects like seas, mountains, and rivers as {{ill|shintai|ja|神体}} (sacred objects). In early shrines, the enshrined deities often had no specific names or were unknown. In texts like the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, and the {{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}, only a few shrines, such as {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|住吉神社}}, mention the names of their deities. Most shrines referred to their deities simply by appending "kami" to the shrine or place name. For example, the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}} lists mostly shrine names without specifying the names of the deities, suggesting that by the early 10th century, most deities did not have formal names.
From around the 10th century, deities began to be associated with specific functions or virtues, such as fire, water, or wood deities, beyond their original roles as tutelary gods of clans or regions. By the late {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the emergence of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹説|Honji suijaku=Honji suijaku|lt=Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu}} (theory of the Buddha as the true nature of kami) and its counterpart, {{ill|Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja|反本地垂迹説}} (Shinto superiority), spurred the personalization of deities. Names were often derived from Japanese mythology, local legends, or combinations of honorifics like "kami" (god), "mikoto" (honorific suffix), "hiko" (prince), or "hime" (princess). Additionally, influential deities such as {{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷}} and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡}} were frequently {{ill|divided spirits|ja|分霊}} and enshrined as main deities.
This trend continued into the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, influenced by the {{ill|Fukko Shinto|ja|復古神道|Fukko Shinto=Fukko Shinto|lt=Restoration Shinto}} movement. By the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) formalized this process, leading to widespread standardization of shrine practices and deity names, even at local and minor shrines.
However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Shikinai shrines|ja|式内社}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).
The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
== Related Terms == * {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体}} * {{ill|Shinzou|ja|神像}} * {{ill|Honzon|ja|本尊}} * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神}}

By the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) formalized this process, leading to widespread standardization of shrine practices and deity names, even at local and minor shrines.
However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Shikinai shrines|ja|式内社}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).
The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
== Related Terms == * {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体}} * {{ill|Shinzou|ja|神像}} * {{ill|Honzon|ja|本尊}} * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神}}

{{Cite sources|date=March 2011}} '''Saijin''' (祭神) refers to the {{ill|deity|ja|神}} enshrined at a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.
== History == Currently, most shrines enshrine deities from {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話}}, or identify their deities as equivalent to those from the mythology.
Originally, {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} began as a form of nature worship, revering sacred objects like seas, mountains, and rivers as {{ill|shintai|ja|神体}} (sacred objects). In early shrines, the enshrined deities often had no specific names or were unknown. In texts like the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, and the {{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}, only a few shrines, such as {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|住吉神社}}, mention the names of their deities. Most shrines referred to their deities simply by appending "kami" to the shrine or place name. For example, the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}} lists mostly shrine names without specifying the names of the deities, suggesting that by the early 10th century, most deities did not have formal names.
From around the 10th century, deities began to be associated with specific functions or virtues, such as fire, water, or wood deities, beyond their original roles as tutelary gods of clans or regions. By the late {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the emergence of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹説|Honji suijaku=Honji suijaku|lt=Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu}} (theory of the Buddha as the true nature of kami) and its counterpart, {{ill|Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja|反本地垂迹説}} (Shinto superiority), spurred the personalization of deities. Names were often derived from Japanese mythology, local legends, or combinations of honorifics like "kami" (god), "mikoto" (honorific suffix), "hiko" (prince), or "hime" (princess). Additionally, influential deities such as {{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷}} and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡}} were frequently {{ill|divided spirits|ja|分霊}} and enshrined as main deities.
This trend continued into the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, influenced by the {{ill|Fukko Shinto|ja|復古神道|Fukko Shinto=Fukko Shinto|lt=Restoration Shinto}} movement. By the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) formalized this process, leading to widespread standardization of shrine practices and deity names, even at local and minor shrines.
However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Shikinai shrines|ja|式内社}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).
The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
== Related Terms == * {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体}} * {{ill|Shinzou|ja|神像}} * {{ill|Honzon|ja|本尊}} * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神}}

From around the 10th century, deities began to be associated with specific functions or virtues, such as fire, water, or wood deities, beyond their original roles as tutelary gods of clans or regions. By the late {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the emergence of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹説|Honji suijaku=Honji suijaku|lt=Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu}} (theory of the Buddha as the true nature of kami) and its counterpart, {{ill|Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja|反本地垂迹説}} (Shinto superiority), spurred the personalization of deities. Names were often derived from Japanese mythology, local legends, or combinations of honorifics like "kami" (god), "mikoto" (honorific suffix), "hiko" (prince), or "hime" (princess). Additionally, influential deities such as {{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷}} and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡}} were frequently {{ill|divided spirits|ja|分霊}} and enshrined as main deities.
This trend continued into the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, influenced by the {{ill|Fukko Shinto|ja|復古神道|Fukko Shinto=Fukko Shinto|lt=Restoration Shinto}} movement. By the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) formalized this process, leading to widespread standardization of shrine practices and deity names, even at local and minor shrines.
However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Shikinai shrines|ja|式内社}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).
The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
== Related Terms == * {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体}} * {{ill|Shinzou|ja|神像}} * {{ill|Honzon|ja|本尊}} * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神}}

{{Cite sources|date=March 2011}} '''Saijin''' (祭神) refers to the {{ill|deity|ja|神}} enshrined at a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}}.
== History == Currently, most shrines enshrine deities from {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話}}, or identify their deities as equivalent to those from the mythology.
Originally, {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} began as a form of nature worship, revering sacred objects like seas, mountains, and rivers as {{ill|shintai|ja|神体}} (sacred objects). In early shrines, the enshrined deities often had no specific names or were unknown. In texts like the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, and the {{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}, only a few shrines, such as {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|住吉神社}}, mention the names of their deities. Most shrines referred to their deities simply by appending "kami" to the shrine or place name. For example, the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}} lists mostly shrine names without specifying the names of the deities, suggesting that by the early 10th century, most deities did not have formal names.
From around the 10th century, deities began to be associated with specific functions or virtues, such as fire, water, or wood deities, beyond their original roles as tutelary gods of clans or regions. By the late {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}}, the emergence of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹説|Honji suijaku=Honji suijaku|lt=Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu}} (theory of the Buddha as the true nature of kami) and its counterpart, {{ill|Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja|反本地垂迹説}} (Shinto superiority), spurred the personalization of deities. Names were often derived from Japanese mythology, local legends, or combinations of honorifics like "kami" (god), "mikoto" (honorific suffix), "hiko" (prince), or "hime" (princess). Additionally, influential deities such as {{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷}} and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡}} were frequently {{ill|divided spirits|ja|分霊}} and enshrined as main deities.
This trend continued into the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, influenced by the {{ill|Fukko Shinto|ja|復古神道|Fukko Shinto=Fukko Shinto|lt=Restoration Shinto}} movement. By the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) formalized this process, leading to widespread standardization of shrine practices and deity names, even at local and minor shrines.
However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Shikinai shrines|ja|式内社}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).
The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
== Related Terms == * {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体}} * {{ill|Shinzou|ja|神像}} * {{ill|Honzon|ja|本尊}} * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神}}

However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|lt=Shikinaisha|12=draft}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).
The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社|12=draft}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿|12=draft}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
== Related Terms == * {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体|12=draft}} * {{ill|Idols in Shinto|ja|神像|12=draft}} * {{ill|Honzon|ja|本尊|12=draft}} * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神|12=draft}}

{{Cite sources|date=March 2011}} '''Saijin''' (祭神) refers to the {{ill|deity|ja|神|12=draft}} enshrined at a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}}.
== History == Currently, most shrines enshrine deities from {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話|12=draft}}, or identify their deities as equivalent to those from the mythology.
Originally, {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft}} began as a form of nature worship, revering sacred objects like seas, mountains, and rivers as {{ill|shintai|ja|神体|12=draft}} (sacred objects). In early shrines, the enshrined deities often had no specific names or were unknown. In texts like the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}, and the {{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集|12=draft}}, only a few shrines, such as {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|住吉神社|12=draft}}, mention the names of their deities. Most shrines referred to their deities simply by appending "kami" to the shrine or place name. For example, the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}} lists mostly shrine names without specifying the names of the deities, suggesting that by the early 10th century, most deities did not have formal names.
From around the 10th century, deities began to be associated with specific functions or virtues, such as fire, water, or wood deities, beyond their original roles as tutelary gods of clans or regions. By the late {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}}, the emergence of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹説|Honji suijaku=Honji suijaku|lt=Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|12=draft}} (theory of the Buddha as the true nature of kami) and its counterpart, {{ill|Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja|反本地垂迹説|12=draft}} (Shinto superiority), spurred the personalization of deities. Names were often derived from Japanese mythology, local legends, or combinations of honorifics like "kami" (god), "mikoto" (honorific suffix), "hiko" (prince), or "hime" (princess). Additionally, influential deities such as {{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷|12=draft}} and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡|12=draft}} were frequently {{ill|divided spirits|ja|分霊|12=draft}} and enshrined as main deities.
This trend continued into the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, influenced by the {{ill|Fukko Shinto|ja|復古神道|Fukko Shinto=Fukko Shinto|lt=Restoration Shinto|12=draft}} movement. By the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) formalized this process, leading to widespread standardization of shrine practices and deity names, even at local and minor shrines.
However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|lt=Shikinaisha|12=draft}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).
The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社|12=draft}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿|12=draft}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
== Related Terms == * {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体|12=draft}} * {{ill|Idols in Shinto|ja|神像|12=draft}} * {{ill|Honzon|ja|本尊|12=draft}} * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神|12=draft}}

{{Cite sources|date=March 2011}} '''Saijin''' (祭神) refers to the {{ill|deity|ja|神|12=draft}} enshrined at a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}}.
== History == Currently, most shrines enshrine deities from {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話|12=draft}}, or identify their deities as equivalent to those from the mythology.
Originally, {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft}} began as a form of nature worship, revering sacred objects like seas, mountains, and rivers as {{ill|shintai|ja|神体|1=Shintai|12=draft}} (sacred objects). In early shrines, the enshrined deities often had no specific names or were unknown. In texts like the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}, and the {{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集|12=draft}}, only a few shrines, such as {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|住吉神社|12=draft}}, mention the names of their deities. Most shrines referred to their deities simply by appending "kami" to the shrine or place name. For example, the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}} lists mostly shrine names without specifying the names of the deities, suggesting that by the early 10th century, most deities did not have formal names.
From around the 10th century, deities began to be associated with specific functions or virtues, such as fire, water, or wood deities, beyond their original roles as tutelary gods of clans or regions. By the late {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}}, the emergence of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹説|Honji suijaku=Honji suijaku|lt=Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|12=draft}} (theory of the Buddha as the true nature of kami) and its counterpart, {{ill|Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja|反本地垂迹説|12=draft}} (Shinto superiority), spurred the personalization of deities. Names were often derived from Japanese mythology, local legends, or combinations of honorifics like "kami" (god), "mikoto" (honorific suffix), "hiko" (prince), or "hime" (princess). Additionally, influential deities such as {{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷|12=draft}} and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡|12=draft}} were frequently {{ill|divided spirits|ja|分霊|12=draft}} and enshrined as main deities.
This trend continued into the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, influenced by the {{ill|Fukko Shinto|ja|復古神道|Fukko Shinto=Fukko Shinto|lt=Restoration Shinto|12=draft}} movement. By the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) formalized this process, leading to widespread standardization of shrine practices and deity names, even at local and minor shrines.
However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|lt=Shikinaisha|12=draft}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).
The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社|12=draft}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿|12=draft}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
== Related Terms == * {{ill|Shintai|ja|神体|1=Shintai|12=draft}} * {{ill|Idols in Shinto|ja|神像|12=draft}} * {{ill|Honzon|ja|本尊|12=draft}} * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神|12=draft}}
{{Shinto Horizontal}}

<!--Saijin-->

However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.

{{Unreferenced|date=March 2011}} '''Saijin''' (祭神) refers to the [[:en:Deity|Deity]] enshrined at a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]].
== History == Currently, most shrines enshrine deities from [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]], or identify their deities as equivalent to those from the mythology.
Originally, [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] began as a form of nature worship, revering sacred objects like seas, mountains, and rivers as [[:en:Shintai|Shintai]] (sacred objects). In early shrines, the enshrined deities often had no specific names or were unknown. In texts like the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], and the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Manyoshu]], only a few shrines, such as [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[:en:Sumiyoshi Shrine|Sumiyoshi Shrine]], mention the names of their deities. Most shrines referred to their deities simply by appending "kami" to the shrine or place name. For example, the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} lists mostly shrine names without specifying the names of the deities, suggesting that by the early 10th century, most deities did not have formal names.
From around the 10th century, deities began to be associated with specific functions or virtues, such as fire, water, or wood deities, beyond their original roles as tutelary gods of clans or regions. By the late [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the emergence of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹説|Honji suijaku=Honji suijaku|lt=Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honji suijaku|qq=}} (theory of the Buddha as the true nature of kami) and its counterpart, {{ill|Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja|反本地垂迹説|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|qq=}} (Shinto superiority), spurred the personalization of deities. Names were often derived from Japanese mythology, local legends, or combinations of honorifics like "kami" (god), "mikoto" (honorific suffix), "hiko" (prince), or "hime" (princess). Additionally, influential deities such as {{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari|qq=}} and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman|qq=}} were frequently [[:en:Bunrei|divided spirits]] and enshrined as main deities.
This trend continued into the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], influenced by the [[:en:Fukko Shinto|Restoration Shinto]] movement. By the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) formalized this process, leading to widespread standardization of shrine practices and deity names, even at local and minor shrines.
However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|lt=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).
The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-Ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/state-Ranked shrines|qq=}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shaden|qq=}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
