{{Unreferenced|date=March 2011}} '''Saijin''' (祭神) refers to the [[:en:Deity|Deity]] enshrined at a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]].
== History == Currently, most shrines enshrine deities from [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]], or identify their deities as equivalent to those from the mythology.
Originally, [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] began as a form of nature worship, revering sacred objects like seas, mountains, and rivers as [[:en:Shintai|Shintai]] (sacred objects). In early shrines, the enshrined deities often had no specific names or were unknown. In texts like the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], and the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Manyoshu]], only a few shrines, such as [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[:en:Sumiyoshi Shrine|Sumiyoshi Shrine]], mention the names of their deities. Most shrines referred to their deities simply by appending "kami" to the shrine or place name. For example, the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} lists mostly shrine names without specifying the names of the deities, suggesting that by the early 10th century, most deities did not have formal names.
From around the 10th century, deities began to be associated with specific functions or virtues, such as fire, water, or wood deities, beyond their original roles as tutelary gods of clans or regions. By the late [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the emergence of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹説|Honji suijaku=Honji suijaku|lt=Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honji suijaku|qq=}} (theory of the Buddha as the true nature of kami) and its counterpart, {{ill|Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja|反本地垂迹説|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|qq=}} (Shinto superiority), spurred the personalization of deities. Names were often derived from Japanese mythology, local legends, or combinations of honorifics like "kami" (god), "mikoto" (honorific suffix), "hiko" (prince), or "hime" (princess). Additionally, influential deities such as {{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari|qq=}} and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman|qq=}} were frequently [[:en:Bunrei|divided spirits]] and enshrined as main deities.
This trend continued into the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], influenced by the [[:en:Fukko Shinto|Restoration Shinto]] movement. By the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) formalized this process, leading to widespread standardization of shrine practices and deity names, even at local and minor shrines.
However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|lt=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).
The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-Ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/state-Ranked shrines|qq=}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shaden|qq=}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
== Related Terms == * [[:en:Shintai|Shintai]] * {{ill|Idols in Shinto|ja|神像|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Idols in Shinto|qq=}} * [[:en:Honzon|Honzon]] * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitogami|qq=}}
{{Shinto Horizontal}}
{{translated page|ja|祭神|version=101180232|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[sn:Saijin]] [[en:Saijin]] [[ja:祭神]]

== Related Terms == * [[:en:Shintai|Shintai]] * {{ill|Idols in Shinto|ja|神像|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Idols in Shinto|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} * [[:en:Honzon|Honzon]] * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitogami|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}

revision.comment: Bot: prepend Wikidata AFC comment via pages.txt
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11591100]] Wikidata name: saijin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=March 2011}} '''Saijin''' (祭神) refers to the [[:en:Deity|Deity]] enshrined at a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]].
== History == Currently, most shrines enshrine deities from [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]], or identify their deities as equivalent to those from the mythology.
Originally, [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] began as a form of nature worship, revering sacred objects like seas, mountains, and rivers as [[:en:Shintai|Shintai]] (sacred objects). In early shrines, the enshrined deities often had no specific names or were unknown. In texts like the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], and the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Manyoshu]], only a few shrines, such as [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[:en:Sumiyoshi Shrine|Sumiyoshi Shrine]], mention the names of their deities. Most shrines referred to their deities simply by appending "kami" to the shrine or place name. For example, the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} lists mostly shrine names without specifying the names of the deities, suggesting that by the early 10th century, most deities did not have formal names.
From around the 10th century, deities began to be associated with specific functions or virtues, such as fire, water, or wood deities, beyond their original roles as tutelary gods of clans or regions. By the late [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the emergence of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹説|Honji suijaku=Honji suijaku|lt=Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honji suijaku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹}} (theory of the Buddha as the true nature of kami) and its counterpart, {{ill|Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja|反本地垂迹説|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹}} (Shinto superiority), spurred the personalization of deities. Names were often derived from Japanese mythology, local legends, or combinations of honorifics like "kami" (god), "mikoto" (honorific suffix), "hiko" (prince), or "hime" (princess). Additionally, influential deities such as {{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神}} and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman|qq=}} were frequently [[:en:Bunrei|divided spirits]] and enshrined as main deities.
This trend continued into the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], influenced by the [[:en:Fukko Shinto|Restoration Shinto]] movement. By the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) formalized this process, leading to widespread standardization of shrine practices and deity names, even at local and minor shrines.
However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|lt=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).
The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-Ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/state-Ranked shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shaden|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社建築}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
== Related Terms == * [[:en:Shintai|Shintai]] * {{ill|Idols in Shinto|ja|神像|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Idols in Shinto|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} * [[:en:Honzon|Honzon]] * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitogami|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}
{{Shinto Horizontal}}
{{translated page|ja|祭神|version=101180232|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Category:Completion tier 4]] }}

Names were often derived from Japanese mythology, local legends, or combinations of honorifics like "kami" (god), "mikoto" (honorific suffix), "hiko" (prince), or "hime" (princess). Additionally, influential deities such as {{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神|1=Inari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q719665}} and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman|qq=}} were frequently [[:en:Bunrei|divided spirits]] and enshrined as main deities.

However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|lt=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.

Names were often derived from Japanese mythology, local legends, or combinations of honorifics like "kami" (god), "mikoto" (honorific suffix), "hiko" (prince), or "hime" (princess). Additionally, influential deities such as {{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神|1=Inari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q719665}} and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman|qq=}} were frequently [[:en:Bunrei|divided spirits]] and enshrined as main deities.
This trend continued into the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], influenced by the [[:en:Fukko Shinto|Restoration Shinto]] movement. By the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) formalized this process, leading to widespread standardization of shrine practices and deity names, even at local and minor shrines.
However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|lt=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).
The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-Ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/state-Ranked shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shaden|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社建築|1=Shinto architecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080012}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
== Related Terms == * [[:en:Shintai|Shintai]] * {{ill|Idols in Shinto|ja|神像|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Idols in Shinto|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shinzō|WD=Q11588751}} * [[:en:Honzon|Honzon]] * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitogami|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=hitogami|WD=Q11376147}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Saijin]][[ja:祭神]][[zh:祭神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11591100]] Wikidata name: saijin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=March 2011}} '''Saijin''' (祭神) refers to the [[:en:Deity|Deity]] enshrined at a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]].
== History == Currently, most shrines enshrine deities from [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]], or identify their deities as equivalent to those from the mythology.
Originally, [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] began as a form of nature worship, revering sacred objects like seas, mountains, and rivers as [[:en:Shintai|Shintai]] (sacred objects). In early shrines, the enshrined deities often had no specific names or were unknown. In texts like the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], and the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Manyoshu]], only a few shrines, such as [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[:en:Sumiyoshi Shrine|Sumiyoshi Shrine]], mention the names of their deities. Most shrines referred to their deities simply by appending "kami" to the shrine or place name. For example, the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} lists mostly shrine names without specifying the names of the deities, suggesting that by the early 10th century, most deities did not have formal names.
From around the 10th century, deities began to be associated with specific functions or virtues, such as fire, water, or wood deities, beyond their original roles as tutelary gods of clans or regions. By the late [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the emergence of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹説|Honji suijaku=Honji suijaku|lt=Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honji suijaku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹}} (theory of the Buddha as the true nature of kami) and its counterpart, {{ill|Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja|反本地垂迹説|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹|1=honji suijaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080781}} (Shinto superiority), spurred the personalization of deities. Names were often derived from Japanese mythology, local legends, or combinations of honorifics like "kami" (god), "mikoto" (honorific suffix), "hiko" (prince), or "hime" (princess). Additionally, influential deities such as {{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神|1=Inari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q719665}} and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman|qq=}} were frequently [[:en:Bunrei|divided spirits]] and enshrined as main deities.
This trend continued into the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], influenced by the [[:en:Fukko Shinto|Restoration Shinto]] movement. By the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) formalized this process, leading to widespread standardization of shrine practices and deity names, even at local and minor shrines.
However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|lt=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).
The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-Ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/state-Ranked shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shaden|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社建築|1=Shinto architecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080012}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
== Related Terms == * [[:en:Shintai|Shintai]] * {{ill|Idols in Shinto|ja|神像|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Idols in Shinto|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shinzō|WD=Q11588751}} * [[:en:Honzon|Honzon]] * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitogami|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=hitogami|WD=Q11376147}}

== Related Terms == * [[:en:Shintai|Shintai]] * {{ill|Idols in Shinto|ja|神像|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Idols in Shinto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shinzō|WD=Q11588751}} * [[:en:Honzon|Honzon]] * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitogami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=hitogami|WD=Q11376147}}

<!--Saijin--><!--Saijin--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fr:Saijin]] [[zh:祭神]] {{wikidata link|Q11591100}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Saijin]][[ja:祭神]][[zh:祭神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11591100]] Wikidata name: saijin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=March 2011}} '''Saijin''' (祭神) refers to the [[:en:Deity|Deity]] enshrined at a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]].
== History == Currently, most shrines enshrine deities from [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]], or identify their deities as equivalent to those from the mythology.
Originally, [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] began as a form of nature worship, revering sacred objects like seas, mountains, and rivers as [[:en:Shintai|Shintai]] (sacred objects). In early shrines, the enshrined deities often had no specific names or were unknown. In texts like the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], and the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Manyoshu]], only a few shrines, such as [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[:en:Sumiyoshi Shrine|Sumiyoshi Shrine]], mention the names of their deities. Most shrines referred to their deities simply by appending "kami" to the shrine or place name. For example, the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} lists mostly shrine names without specifying the names of the deities, suggesting that by the early 10th century, most deities did not have formal names.
From around the 10th century, deities began to be associated with specific functions or virtues, such as fire, water, or wood deities, beyond their original roles as tutelary gods of clans or regions. By the late [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the emergence of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹説|Honji suijaku=Honji suijaku|lt=Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honji suijaku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹}} (theory of the Buddha as the true nature of kami) and its counterpart, {{ill|Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja|反本地垂迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹|1=honji suijaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080781}} (Shinto superiority), spurred the personalization of deities. Names were often derived from Japanese mythology, local legends, or combinations of honorifics like "kami" (god), "mikoto" (honorific suffix), "hiko" (prince), or "hime" (princess). Additionally, influential deities such as {{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神|1=Inari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q719665}} and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman|qq=}} were frequently [[:en:Bunrei|divided spirits]] and enshrined as main deities.
This trend continued into the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], influenced by the [[:en:Fukko Shinto|Restoration Shinto]] movement. By the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) formalized this process, leading to widespread standardization of shrine practices and deity names, even at local and minor shrines.
However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|lt=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).
The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-Ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/state-Ranked shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shaden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社建築|1=Shinto architecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080012}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
== Related Terms == * [[:en:Shintai|Shintai]] * {{ill|Idols in Shinto|ja|神像|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Idols in Shinto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shinzō|WD=Q11588751}} * [[:en:Honzon|Honzon]] * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitogami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=hitogami|WD=Q11376147}}
{{Shinto Horizontal}}
{{translated page|ja|祭神|version=101180232|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Category:Completion tier 4]] }}

Additionally, influential deities such as {{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神|1=Inari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q719665}} and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman|qq=}} were frequently [[:en:Bunrei|divided spirits]] and enshrined as main deities.
This trend continued into the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], influenced by the [[:en:Fukko Shinto|Restoration Shinto]] movement. By the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) formalized this process, leading to widespread standardization of shrine practices and deity names, even at local and minor shrines.
However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|lt=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).
The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-Ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/state-Ranked shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shaden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社建築|1=Shinto architecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080012}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Saijin]][[ja:祭神]][[zh:祭神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11591100]] Wikidata name: saijin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=March 2011}} '''Saijin''' (祭神) refers to the [[:en:Deity|Deity]] enshrined at a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]].
== History == Currently, most shrines enshrine deities from [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]], or identify their deities as equivalent to those from the mythology.
Originally, [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] began as a form of nature worship, revering sacred objects like seas, mountains, and rivers as [[:en:Shintai|Shintai]] (sacred objects). In early shrines, the enshrined deities often had no specific names or were unknown. In texts like the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], and the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Manyoshu]], only a few shrines, such as [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[:en:Sumiyoshi Shrine|Sumiyoshi Shrine]], mention the names of their deities. Most shrines referred to their deities simply by appending "kami" to the shrine or place name. For example, the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} lists mostly shrine names without specifying the names of the deities, suggesting that by the early 10th century, most deities did not have formal names.
From around the 10th century, deities began to be associated with specific functions or virtues, such as fire, water, or wood deities, beyond their original roles as tutelary gods of clans or regions. By the late [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the emergence of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹説|Honji suijaku=Honji suijaku|lt=Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honji suijaku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹}} (theory of the Buddha as the true nature of kami) and its counterpart, {{ill|Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja|反本地垂迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹|1=honji suijaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080781}} (Shinto superiority), spurred the personalization of deities. Names were often derived from Japanese mythology, local legends, or combinations of honorifics like "kami" (god), "mikoto" (honorific suffix), "hiko" (prince), or "hime" (princess). Additionally, influential deities such as {{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神|1=Inari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q719665}} and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman|qq=}} were frequently [[:en:Bunrei|divided spirits]] and enshrined as main deities.
This trend continued into the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], influenced by the [[:en:Fukko Shinto|Restoration Shinto]] movement. By the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) formalized this process, leading to widespread standardization of shrine practices and deity names, even at local and minor shrines.
However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|lt=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).
The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-Ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/state-Ranked shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shaden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社建築|1=Shinto architecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080012}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
== Related Terms == * [[:en:Shintai|Shintai]] * {{ill|Idols in Shinto|ja|神像|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Idols in Shinto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shinzō|WD=Q11588751}} * [[:en:Honzon|Honzon]] * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitogami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=hitogami|WD=Q11376147}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Saijin]][[ja:祭神]][[zh:祭神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11591100]] Wikidata name: saijin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=March 2011}} '''Saijin''' (祭神) refers to the [[:en:Deity|Deity]] enshrined at a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]].
== History == Currently, most shrines enshrine deities from [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]], or identify their deities as equivalent to those from the mythology.
Originally, [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] began as a form of nature worship, revering sacred objects like seas, mountains, and rivers as [[:en:Shintai|Shintai]] (sacred objects). In early shrines, the enshrined deities often had no specific names or were unknown. In texts like the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], and the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Manyoshu]], only a few shrines, such as [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[:en:Sumiyoshi Shrine|Sumiyoshi Shrine]], mention the names of their deities. Most shrines referred to their deities simply by appending "kami" to the shrine or place name. For example, the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} lists mostly shrine names without specifying the names of the deities, suggesting that by the early 10th century, most deities did not have formal names.
From around the 10th century, deities began to be associated with specific functions or virtues, such as fire, water, or wood deities, beyond their original roles as tutelary gods of clans or regions. By the late [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the emergence of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹説|Honji suijaku=Honji suijaku|lt=Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honji suijaku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹}} (theory of the Buddha as the true nature of kami) and its counterpart, {{ill|Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja|反本地垂迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹|1=honji suijaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080781}} (Shinto superiority), spurred the personalization of deities. Names were often derived from Japanese mythology, local legends, or combinations of honorifics like "kami" (god), "mikoto" (honorific suffix), "hiko" (prince), or "hime" (princess). Additionally, influential deities such as {{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神|1=Inari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q719665}} and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman|qq=}} were frequently [[:en:Bunrei|divided spirits]] and enshrined as main deities.
This trend continued into the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], influenced by the [[:en:Fukko Shinto|Restoration Shinto]] movement. By the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) formalized this process, leading to widespread standardization of shrine practices and deity names, even at local and minor shrines.
However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|lt=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).
The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-Ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/state-Ranked shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shaden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社建築|1=Shinto architecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080012}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
== Related Terms == * [[:en:Shintai|Shintai]] * {{ill|Idols in Shinto|ja|神像|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Idols in Shinto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shinzō|WD=Q11588751}} * [[:en:Honzon|Honzon]] * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitogami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=hitogami|WD=Q11376147}}
{{Shinto Horizontal}}
{{translated page|ja|祭神|version=101180232|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Category:Completion tier 4]] }}

Additionally, influential deities such as {{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神|1=Inari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q719665}} and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman|qq=}} were frequently [[:en:Bunrei|divided spirits]] and enshrined as main deities.

Even for {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|lt=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).

By the late [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the emergence of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹説|Honji suijaku=Honji suijaku|lt=Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honji suijaku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹}} (theory of the Buddha as the true nature of kami) and its counterpart, {{ill|Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja|反本地垂迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹|1=honji suijaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080781}} (Shinto superiority), spurred the personalization of deities. Additionally, influential deities such as {{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神|1=Inari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q719665}} and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman|qq=}} were frequently [[:en:Bunrei|divided spirits]] and enshrined as main deities.
This trend continued into the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], influenced by the [[:en:Fukko Shinto|Restoration Shinto]] movement. By the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) formalized this process, leading to widespread standardization of shrine practices and deity names, even at local and minor shrines.
However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|lt=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).
The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-Ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/state-Ranked shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shaden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社建築|1=Shinto architecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080012}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
== Related Terms == * [[:en:Shintai|Shintai]] * {{ill|Idols in Shinto|ja|神像|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Idols in Shinto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shinzō|WD=Q11588751}} * [[:en:Honzon|Honzon]] * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitogami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=hitogami|WD=Q11376147}}

<!--Saijin--><!--Saijin--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fr:Saijin]] [[zh:祭神]] {{wikidata link|Q11591100}} [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Saijin]][[ja:祭神]][[zh:祭神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Saijin]][[ja:祭神]][[zh:祭神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Saijin]][[ja:祭神]][[zh:祭神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11591100]] Wikidata name: saijin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=March 2011}} '''Saijin''' (祭神) refers to the [[:en:Deity|Deity]] enshrined at a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]].
== History == Currently, most shrines enshrine deities from [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]], or identify their deities as equivalent to those from the mythology.
Originally, [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] began as a form of nature worship, revering sacred objects like seas, mountains, and rivers as [[:en:Shintai|Shintai]] (sacred objects). In early shrines, the enshrined deities often had no specific names or were unknown. In texts like the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], and the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Manyoshu]], only a few shrines, such as [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[:en:Sumiyoshi Shrine|Sumiyoshi Shrine]], mention the names of their deities. Most shrines referred to their deities simply by appending "kami" to the shrine or place name. For example, the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} lists mostly shrine names without specifying the names of the deities, suggesting that by the early 10th century, most deities did not have formal names.
From around the 10th century, deities began to be associated with specific functions or virtues, such as fire, water, or wood deities, beyond their original roles as tutelary gods of clans or regions. By the late [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the emergence of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹説|Honji suijaku=Honji suijaku|lt=Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honji suijaku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹}} (theory of the Buddha as the true nature of kami) and its counterpart, {{ill|Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja|反本地垂迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹|1=honji suijaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080781}} (Shinto superiority), spurred the personalization of deities. Names were often derived from Japanese mythology, local legends, or combinations of honorifics like "kami" (god), "mikoto" (honorific suffix), "hiko" (prince), or "hime" (princess). Additionally, influential deities such as {{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神|1=Inari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q719665}} and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman|qq=}} were frequently [[:en:Bunrei|divided spirits]] and enshrined as main deities.
This trend continued into the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], influenced by the [[:en:Fukko Shinto|Restoration Shinto]] movement. By the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) formalized this process, leading to widespread standardization of shrine practices and deity names, even at local and minor shrines.
However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|lt=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).
The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-Ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/state-Ranked shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shaden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社建築|1=Shinto architecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080012}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
== Related Terms == * [[:en:Shintai|Shintai]] * {{ill|Idols in Shinto|ja|神像|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Idols in Shinto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shinzō|WD=Q11588751}} * [[:en:Honzon|Honzon]] * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitogami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=hitogami|WD=Q11376147}}

The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-Ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/state-Ranked shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shaden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社建築|1=Shinto architecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080012}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
== Related Terms == * {{ill|Shintai|en|Shintai|WD=Q327532|lt=Shintai}} * {{ill|Idols in Shinto|ja|神像|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Idols in Shinto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shinzō|WD=Q11588751}} * {{ill|Honzon|en|Honzon|WD=Q11520160|lt=Honzon}} * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitogami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=hitogami|WD=Q11376147}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Saijin]][[ja:祭神]][[zh:祭神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Saijin]][[ja:祭神]][[zh:祭神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Saijin]][[ja:祭神]][[zh:祭神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11591100]] Wikidata name: saijin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:01, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=March 2011}} '''Saijin''' (祭神) refers to the {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|WD=Q178885|lt=Deity}} enshrined at a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}.
== History == Currently, most shrines enshrine deities from {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|lt=Japanese mythology}}, or identify their deities as equivalent to those from the mythology.
Originally, {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|WD=Q812767|lt=Shinto}} began as a form of nature worship, revering sacred objects like seas, mountains, and rivers as {{ill|Shintai|en|Shintai|WD=Q327532|lt=Shintai}} (sacred objects). In early shrines, the enshrined deities often had no specific names or were unknown. In texts like the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}}, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}, and the {{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|WD=Q834321|lt=Manyoshu}}, only a few shrines, such as {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|en|Sumiyoshi Shrine|WD=Q32512|lt=Sumiyoshi Shrine}}, mention the names of their deities. Most shrines referred to their deities simply by appending "kami" to the shrine or place name. For example, the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} lists mostly shrine names without specifying the names of the deities, suggesting that by the early 10th century, most deities did not have formal names.
From around the 10th century, deities began to be associated with specific functions or virtues, such as fire, water, or wood deities, beyond their original roles as tutelary gods of clans or regions. By the late {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, the emergence of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹説|Honji suijaku=Honji suijaku|lt=Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honji suijaku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹}} (theory of the Buddha as the true nature of kami) and its counterpart, {{ill|Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja|反本地垂迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹|1=honji suijaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080781}} (Shinto superiority), spurred the personalization of deities. Names were often derived from Japanese mythology, local legends, or combinations of honorifics like "kami" (god), "mikoto" (honorific suffix), "hiko" (prince), or "hime" (princess). Additionally, influential deities such as {{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神|1=Inari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q719665}} and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman|qq=}} were frequently {{ill|Bunrei|en|Bunrei|WD=Q195793|lt=divided spirits}} and enshrined as main deities.
This trend continued into the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, influenced by the {{ill|Fukko Shinto|en|Fukko Shinto|WD=Q11489378|lt=Restoration Shinto}} movement. By the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) formalized this process, leading to widespread standardization of shrine practices and deity names, even at local and minor shrines.
However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|lt=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).
The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-Ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/state-Ranked shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shaden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社建築|1=Shinto architecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080012}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
== Related Terms == * {{ill|Shintai|en|Shintai|WD=Q327532|lt=Shintai}} * {{ill|Idols in Shinto|ja|神像|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Idols in Shinto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shinzō|WD=Q11588751}} * {{ill|Honzon|en|Honzon|WD=Q11520160|lt=Honzon}} * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitogami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=hitogami|WD=Q11376147}}

<!--Saijin--><!--Saijin--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[fr:Saijin]] [[zh:祭神]] {{wikidata link|Q11591100}} [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Saijin]][[ja:祭神]][[zh:祭神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Saijin]][[ja:祭神]][[zh:祭神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Saijin]][[ja:祭神]][[zh:祭神]]
{{Unreferenced|date=March 2011}} '''Saijin''' (祭神) refers to the {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|WD=Q178885|lt=Deity}} enshrined at a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}.
== History == Currently, most shrines enshrine deities from {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|lt=Japanese mythology}}, or identify their deities as equivalent to those from the mythology.
Originally, {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|WD=Q812767|lt=Shinto}} began as a form of nature worship, revering sacred objects like seas, mountains, and rivers as {{ill|Shintai|en|Shintai|WD=Q327532|lt=Shintai}} (sacred objects). In early shrines, the enshrined deities often had no specific names or were unknown. In texts like the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}}, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}, and the {{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|WD=Q834321|lt=Manyoshu}}, only a few shrines, such as {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|en|Sumiyoshi Shrine|WD=Q32512|lt=Sumiyoshi Shrine}}, mention the names of their deities. Most shrines referred to their deities simply by appending "kami" to the shrine or place name. For example, the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} lists mostly shrine names without specifying the names of the deities, suggesting that by the early 10th century, most deities did not have formal names.
From around the 10th century, deities began to be associated with specific functions or virtues, such as fire, water, or wood deities, beyond their original roles as tutelary gods of clans or regions. By the late {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, the emergence of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹説|Honji suijaku=Honji suijaku|lt=Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honji suijaku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹}} (theory of the Buddha as the true nature of kami) and its counterpart, {{ill|Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja|反本地垂迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹|1=honji suijaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080781}} (Shinto superiority), spurred the personalization of deities. Names were often derived from Japanese mythology, local legends, or combinations of honorifics like "kami" (god), "mikoto" (honorific suffix), "hiko" (prince), or "hime" (princess). Additionally, influential deities such as {{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神|1=Inari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q719665}} and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman|qq=}} were frequently {{ill|Bunrei|en|Bunrei|WD=Q195793|lt=divided spirits}} and enshrined as main deities.
This trend continued into the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, influenced by the {{ill|Fukko Shinto|en|Fukko Shinto|WD=Q11489378|lt=Restoration Shinto}} movement. By the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) formalized this process, leading to widespread standardization of shrine practices and deity names, even at local and minor shrines.
However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|lt=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).
The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-Ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/state-Ranked shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shaden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社建築|1=Shinto architecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080012}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
== Related Terms == * {{ill|Shintai|en|Shintai|WD=Q327532|lt=Shintai}} * {{ill|Idols in Shinto|ja|神像|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Idols in Shinto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=shinzō|WD=Q11588751}} * {{ill|Honzon|en|Honzon|WD=Q11520160|lt=Honzon}} * {{ill|Hitogami|ja|人神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitogami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=hitogami|WD=Q11376147}}
{{Shinto Horizontal}}

Originally, {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|WD=Q812767|lt=Shinto|1=Shinto}} began as a form of nature worship, revering sacred objects like seas, mountains, and rivers as {{ill|Shintai|en|Shintai|WD=Q327532|lt=Shintai|1=Shintai}} (sacred objects). In early shrines, the enshrined deities often had no specific names or were unknown. In texts like the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, and the {{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|WD=Q834321|lt=Manyoshu|1=Man'yōshū}}, only a few shrines, such as {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}} and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|en|Sumiyoshi Shrine|WD=Q32512|lt=Sumiyoshi Shrine|1=Sumiyoshi Shrine}}, mention the names of their deities. Most shrines referred to their deities simply by appending "kami" to the shrine or place name. For example, the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} lists mostly shrine names without specifying the names of the deities, suggesting that by the early 10th century, most deities did not have formal names.
From around the 10th century, deities began to be associated with specific functions or virtues, such as fire, water, or wood deities, beyond their original roles as tutelary gods of clans or regions. By the late {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, the emergence of {{ill|Honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹説|Honji suijaku=Honji suijaku|lt=Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honji suijaku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹}} (theory of the Buddha as the true nature of kami) and its counterpart, {{ill|Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja|反本地垂迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Han-Honji Suijaku Setsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹|1=Honji suijaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080781}} (Shinto superiority), spurred the personalization of deities. Names were often derived from Japanese mythology, local legends, or combinations of honorifics like "kami" (god), "mikoto" (honorific suffix), "hiko" (prince), or "hime" (princess). Additionally, influential deities such as {{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 稲荷神|1=Inari Ōkami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q719665}} and {{ill|Hachiman|ja|八幡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman|qq=}} were frequently {{ill|Bunrei|en|Bunrei|WD=Q195793|lt=divided spirits|1=Bunrei}} and enshrined as main deities.
This trend continued into the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, influenced by the {{ill|Fukko Shinto|en|Fukko Shinto|WD=Q11489378|lt=Restoration Shinto|1=Fukko Shinto}} movement. By the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}}, {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) formalized this process, leading to widespread standardization of shrine practices and deity names, even at local and minor shrines.
However, the historical origins of many shrines remain uncertain. Even for {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki|lt=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, the identification of enshrined deities often relies on oral tradition, leading to "theories of succession" rather than definitive conclusions.
== Main and Auxiliary Deities == Shrines typically enshrine multiple deities. The primary deity is known as the '''main deity''' (主神, *shushin*), while others are referred to as '''auxiliary deities''' (配神, *haishin*) or '''secondary deities''' (相殿神, *aidono shin*).
The distinction between main and auxiliary deities began in the Meiji period with the establishment of {{ill|state-Ranked shrines|ja|官国幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/state-Ranked shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. However, the concept of a "main deity and others" predates this and was expressed through terms like "front" (*mae*) and "secondary shrine" (*aidono*). Auxiliary deities typically have connections to the main deity but may also be included due to historical or political reasons. Some auxiliary deities later became the main deity of a shrine.
The term "aidono" (相殿, or 合殿) refers to a shrine enshrining multiple deities within a single {{ill|shaden|ja|社殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shaden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社建築|1=Shinto architecture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080012}}. When distinguishing between main and auxiliary deities, "aidono shin" refers specifically to auxiliary deities.
